timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Ezekiel

sections.header: Verses 1-5 — Is Vine Wood Better Than Any Other?

heb.paragraph: Israel is frequently compared to a vine in the OT (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21). Normally the image is positive — a vine planted by God. But Ezekiel subverts the tradition: vine wood is utterly useless for construction. A vine has only one purpose — bearing fruit. If it fails to produce fruit, it is good for nothing but burning.

refs.note: Isaiah's Song of the Vineyard: God planted a vineyard expecting good grapes but got wild ones — a failed vine parable that Ezekiel takes further.
Jesus claims to be the true vine. Branches that do not bear fruit are cut off and burned — directly echoing Ezekiel's logic.
God brought a vine out of Egypt and planted it — the positive vine tradition Ezekiel inverts.

notes.note: Vine wood is softer and weaker than forest wood. It cannot be used for building, tools, or even a peg. Israel without covenant faithfulness has no inherent superiority.

Already charred at both ends and scorched in the middle — the vine is already half-consumed (the northern kingdom fell in 722 BC; Judah has been ravaged by deportations). What remains is even less useful.

Calvin stresses that election without obedience produces no inherent worth. Israel was chosen not for inherent superiority but for fruitfulness. Remove the fruit and the vine is inferior to any other wood.
The phrase 'more than any branch among the trees of the forest' sets up the comparison: vine wood vs. timber. The answer is obvious — vine wood is useless for construction.

Block reads the vine parable as a direct assault on Zion theology — the belief that Jerusalem was inviolable because God dwelt there. Ezekiel argues that chosenness without faithfulness is worse than non-election: the vine that fails is burned, not merely ignored.

Zimmerli identifies the parable as a mashal (wisdom saying) that uses artisan logic: a woodworker would never choose vine wood. The rhetorical question expects the obvious answer 'no' — and that answer becomes God's verdict on fruitless Israel.

hist.context: This is the shortest oracle in Ezekiel — a tight parable that strips away Israel's pretensions. The vine was a symbol of national pride, but Ezekiel asks a simple artisan's question: can you make anything useful from vine wood? You cannot even make a peg to hang a pot on. The rhetorical strategy is devastating: Israel's only value was covenant fruitfulness. Without that, the nation is worthless.

heb.paragraph: The double use of the root ma'al intensifies the accusation: Israel has committed 'treacherous treachery' against YHWH. The term is covenantal — it describes violation of a binding agreement, not merely personal sin but treaty-breaking against the divine suzerain.

refs.note: Every tree that does not produce good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire — John the Baptist echoes the vine/fire logic.
Land that produces thorns and thistles is in danger of being cursed and burned — the same agricultural judgment principle.

notes.note: They have come out of one fire and another will consume them — the surviving remnant from earlier judgments will not escape the final judgment. Partial judgment did not produce repentance.
Calvin applies this warning to every covenant community: belonging to God's people guarantees nothing without fruitfulness. Privilege without production leads to destruction.
The recognition formula appears again: 'then you will know that I am the LORD.' Even judgment reveals God's identity and sovereignty.

Block connects the 'fire to fire' motif to the historical sequence: the 605 BC deportation, the 597 BC deportation, and the coming 586 BC destruction. Each 'fire' is an escalation that the survivors failed to learn from.
Zimmerli sees the brevity of the oracle as rhetorically effective: the case against Jerusalem requires no elaboration. The vine image speaks for itself. Brevity communicates finality.

hist.context: The application is blunt: as vine wood is given to fire, so God gives the inhabitants of Jerusalem to fire. They escaped one fire (earlier judgments) only to face another (the final destruction). The desolation of the land demonstrates their unfaithfulness. The vine parable sets up the extended allegory of chapter 16.

rows.label: vv.6-8
rows.text: Application: Jerusalem Given to the Fire

lit.note: A compact, devastating parable: vine wood without fruit is worthless. Israel without covenant faithfulness is fuel for fire.

themes.note: Judgment at 9: the vine is destined for burning. Covenant at 8: the entire parable is about covenant unfaithfulness (ma'al). Mercy at 1: no hope is offered in this chapter.

hebtext.note: Israel is frequently compared to a vine in the OT (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21). Normally the image is positive — a vine planted by God. But Ezekiel subverts the tradition: vine wood is utterly useless for construction. A vine has only one purpose — bearing fruit. If it fails to produce fruit, it is good for nothing but burning.

The double use of the root ma'al intensifies the accusation: Israel has committed 'treacherous treachery' against YHWH. The term is covenantal — it describes violation of a binding agreement, not merely personal sin but treaty-breaking against the divine suzerain.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: This is the shortest oracle in Ezekiel — a tight parable that strips away Israel's pretensions…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Useless Vine

translations.text: And after it is thrown on the fire as fuel and the fire burns both ends and chars the middle, is it then useful for anything?
Behold, it is given to the fire for fuel. When the fire has consumed both ends of it, and the middle of it is charred, is it useful for anything?
“Son of man, how is the wood of a vine different from that of a branch from any of the trees in the forest?
“Son of man, how does the wood of the vine surpass any wood, the vine branch that is among the trees of the forest?

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Vine wood is softer and weaker than forest wood. It cannot be used for building, tools, or even a peg. Israel without covenant faithfulness has no inherent superiority."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin stresses that election without obedience produces no inherent worth. Israel was chosen not for inherent superiority but for fruitfulness. Remove the fruit and the vine is inferior to any other …"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The phrase 'more than any branch among the trees of the forest' sets up the comparison: vine wood vs. timber. The answer is obvious — vine wood is useless for construction."

thread.text: Isaiah's Song of the Vineyard: God planted a vineyard expecting good grapes but got wild ones — a failed vine parable that Ezekiel takes further.
Jesus claims to be the true vine. Branches that do not bear fruit are cut off and burned — directly echoing Ezekiel's logic.
God brought a vine out of Egypt and planted it — the positive vine tradition Ezekiel inverts.
Every tree that does not produce good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire — John the Baptist echoes the vine/fire logic.
Land that produces thorns and thistles is in danger of being cursed and burned — the same agricultural judgment principle.

coaching.tip: The vine wood riddle subverts Israel's self-image. Israel called itself God's vine (Psalm 80, Isaiah 5), but Ezekiel asks: what's vine wood actually good for? You can't build with it, can't hang anything on it. Its only value is fruit. A vine that doesn't bear fruit is literally more useless than any other wood — it can't even function as lumber. The metaphor demolishes privilege without productivity.

Already charred at both ends and burned in the middle (v. 4), the vine is doubly useless — damaged goods that were already purpose-built for only one thing. The allegory is compact but devastating: Israel had one job (bear fruit), failed, and now can't even serve a secondary purpose. Jesus's parable of the vine in John 15 draws on this same tradition — 'apart from me you can do nothing; every branch that does not bear fruit is cut off.'
